data.title: Zillow: FTC Creates Overhang Over Critical Rentals Business (Downgrade)
data.description:
For the most part, large-cap tech stocks have enjoyed an aggressive late summer rally, buoyed by the prospect of rate cuts and continued crowding into the AI trade. One major holdout is Zillow (NASDAQ:Z), the self-described housing super-app.

Zillow stock has fallen ~20% from YTD highs near $90, technically putting the stock in bear market territory. The core driving reason behind the fallout is the FTC's lawsuit against Zillow, particularly in the high-growth rentals business that has carried Zillow's revenue amid a weaker for-sale housing market. The question for investors now is: Can Zillow rebound from here?

I last wrote a buy article on Zillow in August, when the stock was trading near $85 and before the lawsuit had been announced. While I typically think lawsuits are a mere distraction for most companies, I am particularly concerned about fallout impacts that the FTC suit could have on Zillow's Rentals business, which has become the lifeblood of the company's growth. Considering the substantial added uncertainty on this stock, I'm downgrading my position on Zillow to "Neutral."

The FTC suit and why it matters for Zillow's rentals segment

For investors who are unfamiliar, let's briefly round up the major arguments in the FTC's lawsuit against Zillow and potential fallout impacts for Zillow's financials.

In September, the FTC sued Zillow for allegedly paying $100 million to Redfin to exit the rentals business. As most investors are aware, Redfin is now a subsidiary of the Rocket Companies (RKT), which also acquired the Mr. Cooper Group to build a more holistic real estate company with a full brokerage, title, escrow, and mortgage business. The Zillow deal turned Redfin into an aggregator of Zillow's rental listings. Zillow framed the deal as a partnership, but the FTC is claiming it as anticompetitive behavior.

We'll avoid speculating on the case and potential outcomes, since it's a major unknown. The suit is on pause right now amid the ongoing government shutdown, though we note that this provides little respite for investors, as the suit is likely to get picked back up once the government resumes funding.

What we will focus on is the criticality of Zillow's rentals platform to its overall growth strategy. The chart below showcases Zillow's summary results for the first half of FY 2025:

Rentals revenue in the first half grew 35% y/y to $288 million in revenue, which is now 23% of Zillow's overall revenue. However, it represents half of the company's ~$150 million (14%) of y/y revenue growth and has been a larger contributor to growth than the company's core Premier Agent/Residential segment.

Rentals is also more than just a growing piece of Zillow's financials but a core portion of its product strategy. Many headlines this year have focused on the growing popularity of renting, particularly in age demographics that would in the past have entered first homebuying age. This is a function of not only housing unaffordability but also the desire for flexibility to move that has prevailed in our culture since COVID. In this environment, Zillow has sought not to lose mindshare as the first app that consumers go to for all things housing, so its pivot in Rentals has secured its spot as that one-stop shop for real estate.

The outcome of the FTC suit could lead to a sizable settlement, an injunction against anticompetitive behavior, or a rise in rentals competition. No matter what the outcome, it's an added distraction to the growth story for Zillow, especially when Zillow is already dealing with a lawsuit from Compass. As a reminder, the Compass lawsuit stands on similar anticompetitive grounds, accusing Zillow of retaliating against Compass agents on the Zillow platform for their use of private "pocket listings" (whereas Zillow wants every available house to be listed on the MLS and searchable on Zillow's site and app).

Valuation is on the expensive side, given uncertainty

Previously, I had argued that Zillow traded at a relatively rich but fair valuation given its growth profile. Yes, Zillow shares have dropped over the past month, but in my view, not enough to compensate for the added uncertainty for its most critical growth segment.

At current share prices near $71, Zillow trades at a market cap of $17.12 billion. Netting off the $1.16 billion of cash and $230 million of debt on Zillow's latest balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $16.19 billion.

For FY 2025, Zillow is guiding to mid-teens revenue growth. Wall Street consensus pegs this guidance at $2.57 billion in revenue, or 15% y/y growth.

The company is also guiding to "adjusted EBITDA margin expansion" for the full year. If we assume 1 point of y/y expansion (in line with margin expansion trends for the first half of the year) to a 23% full-year margin, adjusted EBITDA would be $591.1 million, up 19% y/y.

This positions Zillow's valuation at 27.4x EV/FY 2025 adjusted EBITDA. Now, for a company that is poised to hit double-digit revenue growth and has support from a potential recovery in the housing market, I'd consider that a fair multiple; and yet, when we stack on uncertainty from not one, but two pending lawsuits, I'm rendered skeptical about this premium.

Key takeaways

Of course, it's not all bad news for Zillow. The prospect of lower interest rates and a hopeful decline in mortgage rates may finally move many prospective homebuyers off the sidelines, putting the focus back on Zillow's core Residential segment. The potential for a rebound in brokerage activity is what keeps me from being overly bearish on this stock; hence, my "Neutral" rating.

At the same time, I'm hesitant to enter a fresh position in Zillow at its ~27x adjusted EBITDA multiple or to buy the dip in this stock when there's the possibility of a large fine or headwind to growth in its Rentals segment. I'm waiting on much more clarity in this name before diving back in; to me, it's best to be on the sidelines for now.

data.title: HTD: Offering A High Level Of After-Tax Total Return, Yet Still Discounted
data.description:
While the AI mania continues to propel tech stocks even higher into October, some caution is advised for investors as more talk of a market top becomes common. The rally has extended into the second week of the federal government shutdown in the U.S., but how long will the rally last, and what will happen when it ends? For income investors like me who are more interested in a steady income stream than making a quick buck from capital appreciation, the answer is to remain patient. Now is not the time to be buying unless you are a momentum trader. But when the dust settles and the next buying opportunity (e.g., market correction) arises, what will you be buying?

If you are an income investor who holds a substantial amount of your portfolio in a taxable account, a tax-friendly CEF that offers high-yield income from relatively safe and steady investments like utilities, financials, communications, and energy companies might just be the ticket. Especially if that CEF offers strong total return potential from a combination of income and a rising NAV, and one that is likely to benefit from lower interest rates going forward. If that sounds like something that interests you, take a closer look at the John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (NYSE:HTD).

An HTD Overview

From the fund’s website, which is very well laid out in my opinion, with all the relevant details summarized and easy to locate, here is a screenshot of the fund objectives and strategy.

Some key facts include total net assets of $946M using about 31% leverage. The inception date of the fund was 2/27/2004 and the fiscal year ends October 31. As of 9/30/25 the fund distribution was 6.43% at NAV and 6.92% at market price. The fund is currently trading at a discount of about -7%, which is near the 52-week average as shown on CEFConnect.

However, the NAV has been rising and although the price has also risen over the past year, the discount remains at -7%. This is one factor that makes the fund a Buy candidate due to the potential for additional capital appreciation whenever the discount begins to close, which is often the pattern with CEFs based on historical trends.

Another factor that gives me reason to believe that further capital appreciation is likely is due to the positive impact of lower interest rates on the utilities sector (XLU). Also, the financial sector (XLF) has been showing strong signs of improving performance over the past year and the fund composition includes the majority of its holdings in those two market sectors.

HTD Portfolio Composition

From the fund’s fact sheet, which was current as of June 30, 2025, about 44% of the fund holdings were in Utilities, and 34% in Financials. The Energy and Communications Services sectors make up the next two largest holdings at 9% and 6%.

Top ten holdings (out of a total of 123 positions) as of 9/30/25 are shown on the fund website.

Electric utilities make up the largest percentage holding by industry at roughly 25% followed closely by 23% invested in banks, according to the fact sheet.

American Electric Power (AEP), currently the largest holding in HTD, delivered a total return of 25% over the past year, outperforming even the S&P 500 despite the latest rally.

That outperformance from electric utilities like AEP is likely to continue well into 2026 and beyond given the outlook for growing energy demands, lower interest rates, and an unabated economic growth outlook. In fact, on the Q2 2025 earnings call, AEP President and CEO William J. Fehrman stated, "We are reaffirming our long-term operating earnings growth rate of 6% to 8%."

Fund Performance and Peer Comparison

Average annual total returns over time have been quite impressive as shown on the fund website. Since inception over 20 years ago the HTD fund has returned 11.7% at NAV as of 9/30/25 and 9.3% at market price. On a YTD basis, the fund’s total return of nearly 19% at market price is well above any of the benchmarks that the fund tracks and better than most of its peer funds.

For example, I recently reviewed another steady monthly income fund that holds a lot of utilities when I wrote about DNP Select Income Fund (DNP). Other peer funds include EVT, UTF, and Reaves Utility Income Trust (UTG). Only UTG has delivered a better total return over the past year.

However, over the past five years, HTD would have been the better choice compared to UTG, at least up until the past month or so.

I am guessing that this recent outperformance from UTG and other utility funds like DNP and HTD is due in part to the effect of lower interest rates on market perception. Investors tend to pile into safe and steady investments like utilities when the economy starts to show signs of slowing growth and when lower interest rates are on the horizon.

As an example of this expected outperformance, Scotiabank analyst Andrew Weisel recently upgraded two of the other top 10 holdings in the HTD portfolio, Duke Energy (DUK) and Entergy (ETR). If we are entering a period of stagflation, as some policymakers suggest, utilities offer one potential safe haven from rising inflation, slowing growth and higher unemployment.

Certain sectors, like health care, utilities, and consumer staples, are less sensitive to economic cycles. Companies in these defensive sectors tend to have more stable demand often leading to a more reliable revenue stream during stagflation.

Reliable, Monthly Dividends

Like the DNP fund that I recently reviewed, HTD offers income investors a steady, reliable monthly dividend that has only been increased over the past ten years with no cuts as shown on the SA Dividend History page.

In fact, the most recent increase was announced in June of this year when the dividend was increased by more than 14% to $0.158 per share from $0.138.

Risks to Consider

With any CEF that holds equities like HTD a broader market correction will most likely cause the NAV to decline as the underlying holdings drop in value. Any hint of higher interest rates in the future, or rising inflation that might cause demand to pull back in the utility sector could also cause NAV destruction.

HTD is a well-managed fund that has seen plenty of market drawdowns in the 20+ years since its inception. According to the fund website, a $10,000 investment in HTD at fund inception in 2004 would be worth more than $67,000 today (with all dividends reinvested).

Depending on when you made your initial purchase, your results may vary, but this illustrates the point often made that staying invested in quality, dividend paying funds even through big market corrections can pay off in the end.

Concluding Thoughts

As I write this review of HTD the stock market is hinting at the possibility of a market correction approaching. As I have suggested in previous articles like the one about DNP and in my recent review of Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI), investors may want to consider shifting or rotating into safer, well managed, high quality dividend paying stocks and funds that invest in more defensive asset classes such as utilities, real estate, energy, and financials. If that is your objective and you agree with what I have written above, you may want to put HTD on your watchlist.

data.title: Sealed Air Corporation: Others Are Beginning To See What I See
data.description:
Back in June of this year, I made the decision to reaffirm Sealed Air Corporation (NYSE:SEE) as a Buy candidate. Even though the company had seen some underperformance relative to the market, driven by declines in revenue, I remained optimistic thanks to its valuation and the fact that management was continuing to push on successfully with significant cost-cutting initiatives. Since that time, financial performance has been mixed. But shares have outperformed the market, rising 13.4%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up 12.7%. A tiny bit of this outperformance came on October 9th, when news broke that the firm had been upgraded by a major analyst. Citing the same cost-cutting moves that I mentioned previously, they clearly see the same kind of potential that I do.

Given how the company stands right now, I think that investors still have the potential for upside. This is true even though the company is not the cheapest candidate out there. But it is, to be clear, on the cheaper end of the spectrum. Of course, this picture can change as new data comes in. And it just so happens that, before the market opens on November 4th, management will be reporting financial results for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, the expectation is for additional weakness when it comes to revenue. Adjusted net profits are forecasted to decline as well. But even with that factored in, shares are priced at levels that I consider to be generally appealing. So unless we see any big changes on that front, I believe that maintaining a Buy rating is appropriate.

Shares make sense

As of this writing, the only new data that we have regarding the health of the company comes from the second quarter of its 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue came in at about $1.34 billion. That's a dip from the $1.35 billion the company reported the previous year. According to management, this decline in revenue was driven by weakness from its Protective segment. Revenue contracted from $451.3 million to $438.9 million.

The picture would have been worse had it not been for foreign currency fluctuations that positively affected those revenues by $5.9 million. The pain, based on the data provided, was largely the result of a $10.3 million hit that the company took from lower volumes and an $8 million hit involving lower prices. Specifically, the firm blamed North America for the volume and price declines, with that being attributable to increased customer churn in its fulfillment portfolio that occurred last year.

By comparison, the Food segment performed much better. Revenue inched up from $893.8 million to $896.1 million. The big driver here was a $14.4 million jump in prices that management was able to achieve, with that attributable mostly to contract formula pricing and U.S. dollar based pricing with customers in Latin America. Unfortunately, lower volumes of $13.1 million offset most of this benefit, with that being the fault of softness in its North American market.

With revenue dropping, a decline in net profits should not be surprising to see. Net income for the company contracted from $97.8 million to $94.2 million. This occurred even though the firm saw a reduction in interest expense from $63.3 million to $55.7 million. It appears as though this pain was largely caused by a decline in the company's gross profit margin from 30.93% to 30.43%. I know that does not sound like much. But it amounts to an extra $9.8 million in pretax pain.

According to management, this hit came from the Protective segment, with a decline in segment profits from $137.5 million to $128.2 million as the company was hit by lower prices and lower volumes that more than offset productivity benefits that the company achieved. It's also worth noting that, year over year, the firm's losses on asset disposals go from $1.3 million to $6 million.

Other profitability metrics for the company were mixed. For instance, adjusted net profits actually grew from $120.7 million up to $131.9 million. We did see operating cash flow drop from $188.2 million to $136.6 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a more modest decline from $180.7 million to $177.9 million. Finally, EBITDA for the business actually improved from $285.5 million to $292.5 million. In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first six months of this year compared to the same time last year. And in the table below, you can see that while the Food segment did experience a decline here, that was only because of a hit caused by foreign currency fluctuations. The real pain was in the Protective the segment as both pricing and volumes affected the company in a negative way.

For the current fiscal year, management remains confident in their previous guidance. They have kept the revenue outlook unchanged at between $5.10 billion and $5.50 billion. They also anticipate EBITDA of between $1.075 billion and $1.175 billion. Operating cash flow is expected to come in at around $600 million, which would likely mean an adjusted reading of about $506.5 million. And according to management, adjusted earnings per share should still come in at between $2.90 and $3.30. At the midpoint, that would get us a reading of approximately $456.9 million.

Using these estimates, we can see in the chart above how shares of the company are currently valued. On a forward basis, they are more expensive than using the figures from 2024 if we rely on the price to operating cash flow approach. But on a price to adjusted earnings basis, they are unchanged, while on an EV to EBITDA basis, they are a bit cheaper. These are multiples that I would consider to be relatively attractive. And relative to other similar firms, the enterprise is trading a bit on the cheap end of the spectrum.

In the table below, you can see precisely what I mean. I stacked this prospect up against five other similar firms. What I found was that, on a price to earnings basis and on a price to operating cash flow basis, two of the five companies ended up being cheaper than it is. Meanwhile, on an EV to EBITDA basis, only one of the firms was cheaper.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Sealed Air Corporation 11.1 10.0 8.1
Sonoco Products (SON) 10.6 7.5 8.6
Graphic Packaging Holdings (GPK) 10.4 7.2 7.2
Ranpack Holdings Corp. (PACK) 2,836.0 12.9 21.1
Avery Dennison Corp. (AVY) 18.1 15.8 11.7
International Paper (IP) 40.5 17.5 16.8

As I stated at the outset of this article, one thing that helped the company on October 9th was that it received an upgrade from a major analyst. This was none other than RBC Capital Markets’ Arun Viswanathan. According to his assertion, there seems to be potential growth in the industrial and electronics markets that could help out its protective packaging division. Strength in Europe and Latin America both could help to boost sales.

But more importantly, there is the opportunity for meaningful cost savings under the CTO2Grow program that the company announced some time ago. That firm expects annual savings to be in excess of $100 million. As I detailed in my previous article about Sealed Air Corporation, annualized savings could be between $140 million and $160 million. These savings will come from a reduction in headcount, certain contract terminations, likely asset sales, and more.

This cost-cutting program won't come cheap. Management currently expects cash costs to total around $160 million. $139 million of those expenses were already incurred as of the end of the second quarter. That leaves only $21 million remaining. But when we factor in non-cash expenses, we are looking at a total cost to shareholders of about $194 million. The fact that most of these costs have already been incurred is encouraging to me. Hopefully, in the second half of this year, we will finally start to see some real improvements from it.

It is worth noting, as I detailed already, that management will be reporting financial results for the third quarter of the 2025 fiscal year before the market opens on November 4th. Leading up to that date, analysts anticipate weaker performance year over year, with revenue expected to fall from $1.35 billion last year to $1.31 billion this year. Earnings per share are forecasted to improve from $0.61 to $0.65, which would take net profits up from $88.7 million up to $95.8 million. But when we look at things on an adjusted basis, which excludes things like restructuring costs, the expectation is a decline from $0.79 to $0.70. That would take adjusted net income down from $115.7 million to $103.2 million.

In the table above, you can also see other profitability metrics for the third quarter of last year. If revenue and adjusted net income do both decline year over year, it's likely that these other metrics will worsen as well. But unless management comes out with a major downward revision for the future, I don't see any reason to be pessimistic.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I maintain that Sealed Air Corporation is an attractive prospect. It might not be the best company out there. But it is one that is fundamentally attractive. Cost cutting initiatives implemented by management should create value for shareholders moving forward. And even though financial performance might worsen in the near term, shares are cheap enough on both an absolute basis and relative to other companies to justify a bullish outlook. Because of that, I have no problem keeping the company rated a Buy for now.

data.title: LG Display Co, Ltd: A Value Opportunity Amid Modest Revenue Outlook
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

I am bullish on LG Display Co., Ltd. (NYSE: NYSE:LPL), driven by the new technology of its display business, the primary RGB Tandem. This is the world’s best OLED technology for the fourth-generation OLED, which achieves 4,000 nits using the industry’s best RGB Tandem technology. The LG primary RGB tandem technology exceeds the industry average, which typically falls within the 1,000 to 2,000 nit range, approximately 50% to 75% lower than LG’s 4,000 nits brightness. LG has achieved a 33% increase in nits technology by utilizing an extra layer of light-emitting material, resulting in a four-stack structure (RG-B-G) instead of the previous three-layer structure, which achieved a peak brightness of 3,000 nits. The new color brightness has now been significantly improved to 40%. With primary RGB Tandem technology, LG is positioned to deliver this screen technology to iPhone 17 and iPad OLED production, which is why there is a forecast of a 70% revenue increase in Q3’2025 from Q2’2025. This optimism in revenue increases is also seen in its YTD price return gain against SP500, which is 75.24% against SP500’s 14.18%.

I will now explore my perspective below to demonstrate why I think this primary RGB Tandem technology is a revenue generator for the LPL, currently making it a Buy.

Company Brief

LG Display, a manufacturer, sells TFT-LCD and OLED panels used in various devices, including TVs, automotive displays, tablets, and computers. This company provides services in venture investments, facility maintenance, and intellectual property management. It was incorporated in 1985 and is based in Seoul, South Korea.

Primary RGB Tandem Growth Catalyst

First things first, I think three reasons drive LPL to create the best displays. One of those reasons is to maintain its lead in the display technology. The second reason is to examine the growth projection of the transparent OLED display market, which is estimated to grow from $3.03 billion as of 2025 to $23.98 billion by 2032. When I examine this growth, which is at a CAGR of 34.47%, and with its new primary RGB Tandem technology, it positions itself to gain more from the market leadership.

In an ever-evolving technological world, maintaining leadership by incorporating new features is a key aspect that LPL has demonstrated over time. One of these is the primary RGB Tandem display technology, which is currently the leading display technology offering 4,000 nits of brightness compared to the average of 1,000 to 2,000 nits.

To provide a picture of what this market leadership looks like, I will use the example of its major customer, Apple, which is the world’s best-selling smartphone. Apple devices have not yet started using LG’s primary RGB Tandem technology, which is scheduled for 2028 when the company is estimated to reach a $5 trillion market volume, assuming there will be no substantial market interruptions. This is what it means for LPL. In 2024, LPL sold 67.5 million panels to Apple, and 42 million of those were used for the iPhone 16 series. In 2025, LPL forecasts to ship over 75.1 million units, of which 45.6 million panels will be used for the iPhone 17 series. Already, the iPhone 16 series is the world’s best-selling smartphone, and this signals that with the sale of 75.1 million units, LPL is headed in the right direction.

Apple plans to enter full-scale mass production of its latest devices, which began in July of 2025, and this is expected to increase the demand for LPL OLED panel shipments. For instance, given that Apple’s iPhone 17 series and new iPad Pro models’ leading supplier is LPL, this is a major driver for a substantial increase in revenues. The planned use of LPL's primary RGB Tandem technology in Apple devices by 2028 is a future that shows LPL’s best positioning to offer the most advanced screen technology to its customers. This explains why the LPL OLED panels shipment is estimated to increase to over 70% in Q3’2025 as the expectation for iPhone panel is forecasted to reach around 18.5 million units in Q3’2025 and 24 million units by Q4’2025.

As such, this optimism reflects the recovery of LPL’s market share for iPhone panel shipments, which had dropped to 21.3% in Q2’2025, marking the first time a Chinese competitor, BOE, had overtaken LPL. With the primary RGB Tandem display technology, LPL is likely to secure more than 30% of total iPhone OLED panel shipments to Apple’s iPhone 17 series and OLED iPad Pro models in 2025 and beyond.

The third reason is a commitment to developing human-friendly screens that do not cause melatonin suppression and visual fatigue. The LED displays cause the two, and eventually affect the circadian rhythm, which affects the quality of sleep. In contrast, OLED minimizes circadian rhythm disruption and is healthier for viewing, which was the primary driver in LPL acquiring the first eyesafe verification for TVs and monitors.

This provides insight into why LPL’s quality of primary RGB Tandem display technology is gaining more orders from the best-selling phones globally, and this is also a reason to be optimistic. These are over 70% of people who spend more than an hour on a screen, indicating eye fatigue. This means that as LPL OLED technology advances to reduce blue light, it is also contributing to decreased eye fatigue. As such, reduced emissions from OLED screens are creating a room for prolonged and comfortable screen viewing without eye strain and blurred vision. This is also a reason I think Apple is switching to OLED displays for all its laptops, tablets, and monitors by 2027, and by 2028, it is likely to start using LPL primary RGB Tandem technology. Apple is showing a lot of interest in LPL tandem OLED because Samsung is behind LG display technology, particularly tandem OLED technology. This is why LG’s OLED panel shipment to Apple is set to increase to 30.3% in Q3’2025 from 21.3% compared to Samsung’s panel shipment expected to drop from 56.05% in Q2’2025 to 53.35% in Q3’025.

Financials & Valuation

LPL has been performing well in terms of revenues, with a solid growth over the last five years, except for FY2022 and FY2023, when the company’s revenues declined due to oversupply of LCD screens.

Additionally, LPL has been investing in OLED display technology, which has resulted in a strategic pivot lag, transitioning from LCD to OLED technology. As such, the transition was not fast enough to offset a steep drop in revenues. This investment in OLED has begun to realize value. For example, the growth of Apple OLED panel orders from LPL from 21.3% in Q2’2025 to 30.3% in Q3’2025, which is an indication of the realized value from the investment in OLED. In other words, the orders in 2024 were 67.5 million units and are estimated to reach 72.1 million units in 2025, representing a 6.81% increase. This is a major driver that led to a 24.77% to KRW26,615,347 million ($18.908 billion) in 2024.

As far as profitability is concerned, LPL is not yet profitable, but what is important here is that the net profit margin is improving YoY, which signals recovery after investing in OLED, which led to a deepened profit decline. Based on my earlier explanations of growth in OLED sales at Apple, I believe this marks the first significant unit sales that the company has achieved, demonstrating optimism in the OLED market, which will drive it into profitability. The net profit margin has improved from -12.82% in 2023 to the trailing profit margin of -2.5%. I believe this trend will continue as the company realizes higher revenues from its investments. By the time the company starts shipping primary RGB Tandem technology, it could turn profitable and sustain profitability from the new revenue stream. OLED is a large-sized business, and this is a major driver that will solidify its leadership in premium OLED panels. For instance, as of Q2’2025, 56% of LPL revenues were from OLED, indicating that the primary RGB Tandem technology is being utilized.

Moving to valuation, in the absence of profits, I will value this company using the comparative P/S multiple. I will compare its P/S ratio to that of Belden Inc. (NYSE: BDC) and Little, Inc. (NASDAQ: LFUS), two of its peers. The P/S ratio indicates that this company is undervalued at 0.27x compared to the BDC P/S ratio at 1.81x and LFUS at 2.83x, which presents a value opportunity here.

With this undervaluation, I will project my price target for this stock and I will begin by estimating its financial performance. In my estimation, I will assume a revenue growth rate of 7% for the 2025 and 8% for the 2026 FYs respectively. Although the company rebounded strongly in 2024 with a double digit revenue growth, the consensus estimates of $18.19 billion in 2025 present a cautious outlook due to tariff-related headwinds. Further, with the trailing revenue growth of 8.96%, I believe my assumptions are rational. Given these assumptions, I am estimating a revenue of about $20.83 billion by 2026.

With this modest revenue growth, I believe the company’s P/S ratio is likely to improve considering that the company is undervalued. While its peer’s average P/S ratio is 2.32x, I will assume a target P/S of 1x which is an improvement from the company’s current 0.27x and lower than the peers' average. This blends both optimism and the possible headwinds. With these inputs, I estimate a price target of about $41.66 by 2026.

With this projection, it is important to note that each ADR only represents 0.5 stock units. With this, I will adjust my price target to reflect this reality. What this implies is that each ADR reflects half the value of one ordinary share. With that, the adjusted target price would be $20.83 which translates to an upside potential of about 287.17%.

Investment Risks

Competition – The display market is a highly competitive sector, with its closest competitor being Samsung, which poses a risk to its future revenue projections. It should be noted that Samsung is investing about $1.8 billion in building advanced OLED factories aiming to dominate the mid-sized OLED segment. With Samsung's scale, I expect better economies of scale, making it more attractive to global electronics brands. This could limit LPL's market reach, potentially limiting its growth relative to Samsung.

Geopolitical risks – The escalating geopolitical risks, such as tariff wars, are pressuring LPL’s business, and this might significantly affect its performance if its global operations are affected. In 2025, tariffs on electronics and components imported from China and Mexico ranged from 10% to 25% impacting supply chains. In my view, this potentially explains why its cost of revenue has increased from $16.28 billion in 2024 to the trailing $17.24 billion. Although LPL benefits from Mexico's tariff exemption through the USMCA, any disruptions to this provision could jeopardize its US market access where 65% of TVs are now sourced from Mexico.

Conclusion

I reiterate my stance that OLED, specifically the primary RGB Tandem technology, is a major growth driver for LPL that will sustain its future growth. I believe this optimism is solid and will drive its revenues in the future.

data.title: HTD: A 7% Yield Machine With Lessons From Past Shocks
data.description:
The John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund (NYSE:HTD) is a decent and consistent monthly payout income CEF. The methodology is active and diversified across more than 100 stocks, primarily in utilities. A ~32% leverage is also employed to magnify returns. The CEF generates income primarily through dividends while also aiming for long-term capital appreciation. There is an emphasis on common and preferred stocks with qualified dividends that ensure better tax implications in taxable accounts.

Overall, I rate HTD as a Buy with caution, driven by its reliable 7-8% yield, strong dividend coverage, and tax-efficient income structure, though high expenses and leverage risks limit capital appreciation potential at this point.

Yield History

HTD usually pays out fixed, targeted monthly dividends. The current dividend is $0.158 monthly, which translates to ~7.66% yield on current prices. The dividend is usually growing, with a five-year growth rate of 0.71% recorded in the most recent five-year period. The yield usually fluctuates between 7% and 10%, mostly peaking in regimes of high interest rates. The yield is quite healthy and stable, even on a cost basis.

NAV Erosion

Despite the growing and stable dividend yields and a leveraged position, I do not see any systematic NAV erosion in the past decade. There are cyclical patterns in the prices, though, so when one enters HTD matters. The total returns in the past five years are a very healthy ~82%, indicating capital appreciation even after accounting for an annual yield of 7-8% on average. However, that is because this five-year period starts near the COVID lows. If you stretch the starting point to January 2020, the total returns turn out to be only ~40%, enough to account for the yield, at best. The current share prices are towards the higher end of their range as seen in the last decade. Entries now may not gain from capital appreciation, and the Buy thesis will be based only on the dividend promise. The last time the odds were stacked in favor of capital appreciation was in April 2025 during the tariff sell-off or, even better, before 2024, after the rate hike era in 2022-23.

The share prices react to rates, sector rotations, and premium/discount dynamics resulting therefrom. Often a wider than average discount to NAV is an indication of a good time to get in, because it coincides with rate shocks or sector sell-offs. The current NAV is at $26.62, and the prices are pegged at $24.74, which is a reasonable discount of ~7%, similar to levels seen in 2024. The discount was wider at ~9.5% a couple of years back in 2023 and narrower at ~2.5% in 2022. The current discount levels are not factoring in aggressive rate cuts yet that are supportive for utilities. For longer term income investors, that is not a bad time to enter HTD if the horizon is more than a full interest rate cycle spanning 5-10 years.

So, although prices are near historical highs, the discount looks reasonable for fresh entries now. As a general rule, I ideally look for conditions as close to post-rate-hike-related pullbacks or early rotation into utilities/income themes, crises or broad market lows, or even after initial rate cuts, as is the case now, for fresh entries.

Portfolio Specifics and Leverage Risks

The Q2 2025 holdings look heavily tilted towards utilities, although financials also occupy decent weightage in the HTD portfolio. A deeper dive into its holdings reveals up to one fourth of the exposure concentrated in electric utilities, with banks coming in a close second in terms of industries HTD is invested in. The only other double digit allocation comes from multi utilities. The portfolio is obviously skewed towards dividend and income names, often cyclical in nature.

The top 10 holdings account for a little above 30% in a portfolio of around 100+ names. That is decent diversification. The top allocations are themselves in the vicinity of ~3.5%, exposures that do not make or break the portfolio on their own, unless a broader market wide unfavorable theme unfolds.

In cases of broader market pressures, such as last seen in the COVID sell-off in 2020, the leverage does lead to significant NAV erosion. The COVID sell-off saw almost a 56% total return fall in a matter of a couple of months alone. That is the last crisis we saw where liquidity issues and market panic were at a peak, and HTD's leveraged stand took a beating. That kind of sell-off was not matched even in a more pertinent rate shock of 2022-24, where HTD stagnated for a while and corrected by up to 30% in total return terms. Investors looking at HTD as a long-term vehicle should be ready to double up in such drawdowns that are likely to appear in adverse market conditions on HTD's leverage.

Overall, the risk return profile does seem to suffer because of the protracted drawdowns and leverage. The fund generally bounces back eventually and keeps payouts intact. It even keeps the total return profile flat for most parts in adverse scenarios, such as the 2022-23 period where rates were pressuring the NAV, but payouts kept the total returns above water.

Expenses and Tax Treatment

The expense ratio is extremely high for HTD at ~4.44%, as mentioned in its factsheet. However, the tax efficiencies are material. I downloaded the 19(a) distribution notice for HTD and found around 71% of YTD payouts classified as qualified dividends. ~15% of the income is classified as long-term capital gains and an equal amount as short-term gains. There is no distribution classified as ROC. The ROC portion shows up only in September, with 19% payouts attributable to potentially tightening income coverage. Investors should keep an eye on rising ROC components in payouts (monthly reports are released to keep an eye on). Since the management tries to maximize tax efficiencies, a rising ROC component is a warning for both tax friendly payouts as well as tightening income generation environments from leveraged qualified dividends.

Hold Bordering on Buy

For investors already invested in HTD, I think the call is a Hold because payouts remain well covered. Any rising ROC component could signal offloading a bit if required. On the other hand, any sectoral sell-off or discount widening could be better times for entries.

For new investors, HTD is a Buy with enough firepower to ride out a full rate cycle spanning several years and averaging entries on corrections. Overall, HTD appears well positioned to maintain distributions through cycles, as its strong historical dividend and NAV stability have shown, particularly for investment horizons of around 5-10 years. The portfolio is well diversified, and the ROC component appears well controlled at this point. Tax efficiency is clearly a focus in the long term and should support the decent payout while keeping the NAV intact on average. The only thing in my watchlist that prevents a Strong Buy call is the high expense ratio at a point when the NAV is at historical highs, limiting capital appreciation potential. I am also wary of the leverage risks as seen in adverse markets.

data.title: UPM-Kymmene: Why I Don't Mind The Longer Downward Trend
data.description:
I've been covering UPM-Kymmene (OTCPK:UPMMY) for a few years at this point - but only relatively recently started more heavily investing in the company. This company is a mixed forestry, paper, and biofuel company. I would go so far as to characterize it as one of the foremost such companies in all of Europe, with significant growth potential, excellent profitability, and superb assets and portfolio. Its main challenges lie in the various end markets, which are somewhat cyclical, as well as recent results, momentum, and expectations.

This can also be highlighted or seen in the Seeking Alpha quant ratings, found here.

Quant has the company at a "HOLD" - while Wall Street analysts consider it to be a clear "BUY" here. I agree with the latter, and in this article, I will go ahead and justify this as well as see if an update of my price target and assumptions is valid at this point.

As an investor, I don't mind lengthy downturns. While in the scenario where I am in the negative, this calls into question at what valuation I invested, I usually have a high conviction that the company's recovery will come.

But recent results imply that the recovery, at least for the time being, isn't as clear as we might like.

You can find my last article here, where I also had a "BUY" rating with an expectation of a 15%+ annualized upside for the longer term. I already, at that time, considered the company unsuitable for shorter-term investments.

UPM Kymmene - Upside is there, but results are so-so in the light of a difficult macro and trading environment

The company's most recent results were okay, given the period that we're looking at. 2Q25 tends to be one of the weaker periods for the company, typically, and quarterly sales totalled €2.4B, which was down from YoY €2.5b. The EBIT decrease of 31% likely also contributed significantly to the downturn I valuation, even if OCF didn't decline even half as much.

These poor trends were related primarily to poor global trade flow and global tensions, as this industry's end markets are quite cyclical and dependent on macroeconomic conditions. This goes doubly for the Fibres and the communications papers segment - with advanced materials and biofuels being correlated to other markets, or showing more resilience.

The company made some small changes in its strategy - such as the decision to discontinue the Rotterdam refinery development, instead sharpening growth in biofuels in other ways. This does not mean that UPM is lowering its refining capacity, as its refinery in Leuna has started up already as of the 1H25 period.

To summarize the headwinds the company is currently facing, we're talking about a weaker US dollar, which greatly impacts UPM, volumes at lower prices and lower levels, low pricing for electricity (especially in the home market of Finland), low demand for communication papers, customer decisions affected by the current trade and tariff uncertainty, and the like. From an EBIT perspective, volume declines and fixed cost increases was the main reasons why EBIT was down as much as it was, and by segment, we can zero in on the weakness specifically to fibres, energy, and communications papers (also plywood, though that has other reasons).

These poor recent results have obviously inflated the company's leverage. It means that the current net debt/EBITDA is actually above the company's policy target of 2x, at 2.12x, but this is likely to be momentary. Furthermore, UPM is not burdened by financial covenants - the limit is of a self-imposed nature, and the company has cash/equivalents of over €2.5B on hand. In fact, it even took advantage of the low valuation and repurchased shares of €160M in 1H25, which should convey how much of a non-issue they view this momentary downturn to be.

Important positives for the quarter include the biorefining and biofuels process. The company's biofuel segment reached an EBIT break-even in 2Q, with significant delivery increases coupled with lower fixed costs. This is, in fact, before it has reached full production, so once it does, it's expected to improve even more.

The reason why tariffs and global trade impact the company to the degree that they do has to do with the portion of imported relative to locally produced fibre and product in the NA geography. For UPM, the balance is still tilting the other way, with a majority of sourcing being done from imports. Indirectly, tariffs and the like have caused, above all, hesitation for many customers to commit. This is obviously something we also see in other areas of the market. Specifically, when it comes to the US market, the company imports finished products in the form of communication and specialty papers, as well as eucalyptus pulp, all of which are potentially targeted by tariffs and the like.

Outlook on a medium or short-term basis is muted compared to two years ago. The company now, as of 2H2025, expects EBIT in the range of €650M on the high end, which is to be compared to just over €700M in 2H24 alone. This would signify a significant decline.

However, 2H25, compared to the poor 1H25, is expected to bring better results (as is the typical pattern we can also see above), from lower variable costs, certain timings, and energy refunds, which will add to results. Furthermore, the resilience we've seen in some of the company's segments is also likely to continue, with a net result of at least "okay" EPS for the year.

What has been driving the company's recent results trends is the fact that the company has been in the midst of a major investment cycle. The company's CapEx has been significant basically since 2020, and has only started winding down towards the 2024 and now the 2025 period. It's hard for me to convey in words how significant the impact of this will become if and when the company's investments start paying off, while the necessity to fund further investments declines. Consider the company's history as shown below with regard to investments.

When the company was last in a period where CapEx was declining like that, and when operational investments were the only things that were necessary, the valuation for the company was very different from what you see today. There's no doubt in my mind that the company will continue to trade low until we see fundamental improvements - but just like that, there are also no doubts in my mind that eventually, those improvements will come once things turn around.

My cost basis for this investment is now below €26/share. That gives me a yield basis of close to 6%. UPM Kymmene is, next to some other local investments, also the by far largest investment that I currently hold, at almost 4% in both my private and my corporate account/portfolio. That is the depth of my conviction here.

I remind you that during good times, the company's share price was well over €32/share - and I believe eventually, it will go back to this level. This is a clear downturn, but while you're waiting for it to reverse, you're getting paid a very attractive dividend to do so, and even based on 2024-2025 targets, that dividend is paid by EPS.

I further believe that the company's assets, the forests that make up its portfolio, will only increase in value over time. The Uruguay plantations are already valued at close to €2B, and productivity here is significant, while its home-market advantages in Finland show proof of the valuation increase. The value of its forests in Finland has increased by more than 40% in less than 16 years, signifying a. EUR/hectare increase of 140%.

Eventually, such numbers and trends will trickle down to company valuation. Remember also that UPM owns its own electricity generation, which is already dialled in to meet every climate and ESG goal out there, with a combination of low-CO2 assets.

As before, it's the combination of assets that appeals to me here.

This brings us to the following valuation.

Valuation for UPM Kymmene - attractive here, with significant upside at a €22/share price, which does not worry me.

One of the biggest questions I had for myself here is whether I should reflect the short-term uncertainty in the company in the longer-term price target, which, as of the last article, I maintain at €35/share. I ultimately decided against this, as doing so would not reflect my underlying thesis for the company.

That being said, the "time to maturity" here is the thing to be aware of, for sure.

The reason for my update here, and what's changed since my last article, is the company's valuation. Specifically, for those interested in the company, it has significantly improved in terms of yield and potential upside.

But what happens during that maturity is the core argument for my investing. Based on biofuel assets coming online, based on better efficiency, lower CapEx (which is proven going forward, the company isn't going to be investing as much), coupled with a market recovery, the scenario I see is characterized by a number of trends. First off, more revenue growth. Averaged about 0.5-0.7%. I put it at 1-1.5% per year on a forward, 2026-2028E basis, even with the decline in communication papers. Secondly, lower CapEx and better trends, with more earnings from resilient segments, will increase the company's operating margin - I put it at a 100-150 bps improvement from the 5-10 year average of around 13.7%, which puts it at 15%. Thirdly, P/E. Here I'm more conservative, because of, among other things, some peers (though none of those peers are currently more attractive than UPM).

Obviously, it's better if I also provided an illustration of what could happen, so see below.

That is basically the result of my assumptions. I further assert that the potential for the company to do a lot better than expected is high, because the company, on a 1-year basis, with a 10% margin of error, outperforms or hits targets more than 78% of the time, beating them over 60% of the time. This company has a tendency to be undervalued, and that works in its favor.

Combining low debt with a good dividend, good earnings, and a more virtuous cycle going forward due to lower CapEx, I'm perfectly happy continuing to put money to work in UPM Kymmene when capital is available. Lowering my target would be too short-term. I maintain a fair value of around €40/share, which makes a PT of €35/share for the long-term relevant.

Peers do not have better outlooks, in my view. Stora Enso (OTCQX:SEOJF) is one alternative, and its current P/E is 60x with a yield of less than 2.8%. Swedish SCA is another, trading at almost double the P/E of UPM, but with a yield below 2.5%. (though SCA, if dropping more, could certainly become an interesting investment too - it's on my list).

Forest and paper, as well as biofuels, are currently one of the most attractive investment potentials. For that reason, I am putting capital to work here. The company's recent improvements in its fundamentals, including slowly coming out of the high CapEx period (including the improvements we're seeing in biofuels, which aren't reflected in the valuation we're currently seeing. It's this valuation change, together with the company's fundamentals, that justifies the update here.

I see the following risks with my thesis on UPM.

Risks & Considerations

I would still argue that the primary risk here is actually the volatility and cyclicality of the underlying end markets that UPM works with, as we've seen in 2Q25. Part of why the update is merited though, is that i view these risks as a bit more "clear" now and with a bit more safety, given both the price and the biofuel results. That they go up and down is apparent from the company's trading/valuation trends. The company's exposure to biofuels and biochemicals remains a core growth aspect that has yet to pay off, and the company confirms this in its latest earnings release as well. and energy is as volatile as ever, especially when some of it is renewable.

However, with the company's fundamentals solid, I would be clear that the sole risk worth noting here is not necessarily company-individual on an operational basis, but more operational on a worldwide/sector basis, in that these markets are uncertain. The primary risk would, as I see it here, be towards more short-term investors, because it's not inconceivable that UPM goes down even more.

Both in terms of peers and in terms of historical upside, as well as the current market conditions for the 3-5 year period, UPM is attractively priced and positioned, which lends itself well to investing for the medium to longer term.

The ADR for UPM is UPMMY, which is a 1/1 ADR, meaning my PT of €35/share comes to about $39/share.

My thesis for the company, updated for 2Q25, is as follows.

Thesis

UPM is one of the world's best and most appealing, Finnish-based forestry and pulp/chemical companies with a good energy arm. It also has a great yield of over 6% at this particular time. My position in the company is now very significant, and I'm not scared of buying more either.

I'm as of 2Q25 reiterating my rating on the company as a "BUY" here, with a price target of at or around €35/share-though at €35/share, the upside is barely double digits, and the company could easily go higher to €40-€45 for the native ticker (closer to my fair value as of this time)

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 2 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of €35/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of October 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EWT: Taiwan Exposure With Tech Overload And Geopolitical Risk
data.description:
Taiwan is a country I'm familiar with, as I've lived there and traveled to the beautiful island multiple times thereafter. If you can only travel to one country in this world, Taiwan would be at the top of my list. But since we're not here to discuss travel destinations, let's focus on the positives that the country has to offer for investors.

Investing in Taiwanese stocks can give us access to a dynamic and innovative economy, especially in the tech sector. With a population of just 23 million people, it belongs to one of the top 25 economies in the world.

Unlike China, Taiwan stands out as the second most democratic country in Asia, just after Japan. So, there's a lot to like about the country.

The iShares MSCI Taiwan ETF (NYSEARCA:EWT) is a convenient way to capture the growth potential of the country, but there are also substantial risks. EWT seems to be a concentrated bet on the country's volatile and cyclical tech sector, as the largest holding makes up almost one-quarter of the assets invested.

Other factors we should include in our intrinsic value calculation are the constant geopolitical challenges, most importantly the tense China-Taiwan relations. Given these risks, EWT's current valuation doesn't offer a margin of safety. I therefore rate it a "Hold." Let's dive deeper into EWT by starting with the basics.

Shares MSCI Taiwan ETF

According to iShares, EWT offers exposure to large- and mid-sized companies in Taiwan. It tracks the MSCI Taiwan 25/50 index, which has specific limits to prevent significant concentration. No single company should exceed 25% of the index. Right now, EWT's largest holding comes close.

The expense ratio is 0.59%, which is in line with other ETFs that express a single-country view. However, if we compare it to the Franklin FTSE Taiwan ETF (FLTW) with its 0.19% net expense ratio, EWT's fees seem high.

The turnover rate is 24%. As a long-term investor, I'd prefer it to be lower, but it's still acceptable. The higher the turnover, the more an ETF rotates its holdings and the higher the transaction fees.

P/E Ratio

Yahoo Finance claims the P/E ratio is 19.48, while the Wall Street Journal puts it at 21.43. The WSJ numbers tend to be quite accurate on a TTM basis, so I tend to rely on them.

A 21x P/E ratio seems more attractive than U.S. stocks, which trade at an average P/E of 31x right now. Taiwan's valuations are similar to other developed markets like Germany, the U.K., and Japan. If we compare it to the country's historical valuations, it's neither expensive nor a bargain right now.

Taiwan Faces Geopolitical Risks

A couple of years ago, at a time when others deemed the market uninvestable, I decided to invest aggressively in Hong Kong and Chinese shares. At that time, one could find many highly profitable companies trading at single-digit P/E ratios with near double-digit dividend yields.

At the height of the negativity, I recommended Chinese shares to friends and relatives, yet only a few took advantage of this opportunity, and those that did only bought cautiously.

The ones who refrained from buying into the fear were quick to tell me about the risks that come with investing in China. The relationship with Taiwan and the inevitable invasion that was sure to follow was most often stated.

Here we are, a couple of years later, and the talk about an imminent Chinese invasion has quieted down, but the risk is still there. Yet, when it comes to Taiwanese equities, investors don't seem to worry as extensively, even though it could have disastrous effects on investors in the country.

When valuations are low (and many risks have been priced in), investors tend to panic. And when valuations are relatively high, and therefore more risks prevail, investors tend to worry less, which of course is irrational.

Similar risks that Hong Kong and Chinese stocks face also apply to Taiwanese stocks. At a 21x P/E ratio, those risks don't seem to be fully priced in.

Dividend Yield

As a long-term investor, I like to look at long-term trends. EWT's current dividend yield is 2.66%, but over the cycles, the distributions tend to be volatile.

Taiwan is regarded as an emerging market, just like South Korea. But having lived in Taiwan, it's difficult for me to classify it as a developing country. Just like South Korea and Japan, I'd describe it as a developed country.

Unlike most emerging markets, Taiwan's currency, the Taiwan dollar, is relatively stable compared to the U.S. dollar.

Therefore, currency exchange rates can't be the determining factor when it comes to the volatility in EWT's distributions. It has to do with the sector allocation and the underlying holdings, which we're going to cover now.

Sector Allocation

EWT bets heavily on tech, as the sector represents 66.53% of total assets.

It's followed by financials, industrials, and consumer cyclical. It's fair to say that EWT is a concentrated bet on Taiwan's tech sector and therefore shouldn't be a major part of a well-diversified portfolio.

We're living in extraordinary times, and I'm skeptical when it comes to the valuations of the more popular tech stocks. It seems like no price is too high for the most promising highfliers.

The higher the prices rise, the more investors seem to forget that the tech sector tends to be cyclical and volatile over the cycles.

Top Holdings

EWT holds 95 stocks, and the top 10 holdings make up 48.13% of the assets. That's quite concentrated.

The top holding, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC), represents 23.96% of total assets, which to me seems risky.

As a concentrated investor myself, I don't mind betting big on my best ideas, but if I were to invest in ETFs, I'd want as much diversification as possible, and EWT doesn't deliver that.

Top Holdings: P/E Ratio and Growth Rates

When it comes to analyzing single-country ETFs, a popular approach is to focus on the macro environment or the economic data of the country. But after all, it's the profitability of the underlying holdings that will determine the long-term returns of an ETF.

Therefore, we're going to dive deeper into the top holdings with an emphasis on valuations and quality.

Company: P/E Ratio: Annual EPS Growth Rate (Past 5 Years):
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing 34.38 25.70%
Hon Hai Precision Industry 18.77 6.90%
Delta Electronics 56.14 9.50%
MediaTek 19.79 31.40%
Quanta Computer 17.12 24.90%
Fubon Financial Holdings 10.33 7.80%
CTBC Financial Holding 11.86 8.90%
Accton Technology 35.85 21.10%
Cathay Financial Holding 11.78 27.17%
Asustek Computer 13.91 6.80%

(GuruFocus.com)

The average P/E ratio of the top holdings is 22.99. That's roughly in line with the WSJ numbers provided earlier. The average annual EPS growth rate is 17%. Overall, the quality of the top holdings is decent, but the valuations already more than fully reflect the quality.

What the valuation doesn't reflect is the cyclicality of the tech sector, especially the semiconductor sector.

Cyclicality of the Semiconductor Sector

With almost one-quarter of assets invested, TSMC is a major part of EWT. In the current exuberant market environment, everyone seems to be betting on tech and semiconductors at elevated multiples, but the risks shouldn't be dismissed.

First, TSMC is trading at a 34x P/E ratio. For a company in a highly cyclical sector, that's a steep valuation. The high cyclicality shows in TSMC's cash flows over the decades.

Just like other companies in the sector, TSMC goes through boom and bust cycles. Right now, it seems like we're entering the euphoria phase, which is likely going to be followed by another period of disappointing profitability and returns. "Trees don't grow to the sky" is a famous saying, but the more prices rise, the more investors forget.

The time to buy into cyclical sectors is during bear markets, when profitability and earnings multiples are low. Investors who extrapolate past performance into the future will likely face a painful reality check in the years ahead.

Given the concentration in tech (and especially TSMC), the valuations, and geopolitical risks, I rate EWT a "Hold." I wouldn't initiate a long-term position at current valuations.

Performance Since Inception

EWT has been around since June 2000. It was right after the time the dot-com bubble burst. Let's see how EWT fared over the long term.

Over the last 25 years, EWT has gained 285.6% on a total return basis, for a CAGR of just 5.56%. Just like the NASDAQ, the ETF saw dismal returns during the lost decade from 2000 to 2009.

It was a challenging time for tech stocks because of the hype and overvaluation in the late 1990s. I see similarities in today's environment.

Semiconductor and tech stocks tend to be cyclical and, given the fast-changing technology and highly competitive environment, also prone to disruption. Long-term Intel investors would probably agree.

At current valuations, there are plenty of risks, but the more tech stocks rise, the less investors seem to care.

Comparison Against Other ETFs

Let's quickly compare EWT to peers. The only competitor seems to be the Franklin FTSE Taiwan ETF (FLTW). Since its inception in December 2017, it has vastly outperformed EWT on a total return basis.

FLTW comes with a relatively low net expense ratio of 0.19%. It's a little less concentrated in TSMC and could be a good alternative for investors interested in Taiwanese equities.

Conclusion

Having lived in Taiwan, it's easy for me to admire the country, both culturally and economically. There's no denying the impressive growth and innovation coming out of its tech sector, and with its large allocation, EWT offers a front-row seat to that.

But investing isn't about admiration; it's about risk and reward. At current valuations, I just don't see enough of the latter to justify the former.

Just like in China, investors in Taiwan face geopolitical risks. It's a tail risk, but it could end up being catastrophic for investors in EWT. At current valuations, this doesn't seem to be priced in. The heavy concentration in TSMC, which is trading at elevated multiples, also exposes us to the cyclicality of the semiconductor sector.

There may come a time when valuations fall and the risk/reward shifts in our favor. Until then, I rate EWT a "Hold."

data.title: EUAD: Incursions In EU Highlight Need For Drone Defense And Rearmament
data.description:
I imagine most investors have a list of instincts and insights they wish they acted on. Certainly, that’s the case for me, and one of my most recent regrets was failing to dig into drone stocks before Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth announced that the Department of Defense would be focusing on drone warfare and defense going forward. I wrote an article on the Global X Defense Tech ETF (SHLD), a diversified global ETF, before the announcement, noting that drones were likely to be a strong catalyst, and I had intended to dig more deeply into drone stocks. Alas, I got sidetracked by this and that, and the announcement came more quickly than I anticipated, and I largely missed out on a huge drone stock surge, as illustrated below. I believe, however, that the Select STOXX Europe Aerospace & Defense ETF (BATS:EUAD) offers another opportunity to cash in on drones and drone defense. Individual companies, including Thales (OTCPK:THLEF), Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF), and Rheinmetall (OTCPK:RNMBF), could do particularly well if they have a stronger presence in drone warfare and defense.

Mysterious Drones Flock Across Europe

So far, at least 15 airports across Europe have been forced to shut down (temporarily) due to mysterious drone incursions. At least 17 flights have been diverted as well. It’s widely believed that Russia is behind the drone incursions, and it’s easy to see why. Russia is embroiled in an intense and draining war in Ukraine. Much of Europe, meanwhile, has been providing aid and weapons to Ukraine, drawing Russia’s ire. Russia has been accused of espionage attacks on European soil, including setting fire to a German defense company’s facilities.

So far, however, European leaders can’t definitively prove that the latest airport incursions are at the behest of Russia. For its part, Russia has denied involvement. That said, NATO planes have had to escort Russian military jets out of Estonian airspace, and several Russian drones staged an incursion into Poland, with some being shot down. Still, many of the drone sightings in recent weeks have been at airports a long way from Russia, leading to questions of how Russia or another party is able to launch and recover them. Ukraine claims that Russia’s shadow fleet of oil tankers is being used to launch the drones.

Last fall, drone sightings, especially near military bases, became common in New Jersey and along the East Coast. It is important to keep in mind that reports of drone sightings will encourage more eyes looking up into the skies, which in turn could lead to people misidentifying planes and other objects and reporting them as mysterious drones. It’s also possible that hobbyists are responsible for some of the drone incursions.

European Leaders Call for a Drone Wall

In the face of the threat posed by Russia and pressure exerted by the Trump Administration, Europe has been shifting towards rearming. Most European members of NATO have pledged to spend 5% of their GDP on military projects, with 3.5% meant to be spent on “hard” military assets, while the rest can be spent on projects related to military needs (such as increased cybersecurity.) Europe's rearming should boost Europe’s defense industry. That said, with many European countries facing high debt, sluggish economies, and aging/shrinking populations, I have had concerns over long-term commitments. The recent drone incursions, however, should provide some powerful tailwinds.

European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen has called for Europe to develop a so-called drone wall, or a layered network of anti-drone and aerial defenses, to protect from drone incursions launched by Russia and/or other entities. Yet Europe is a vast region, and if Russia can use tankers and other ships to launch drones, defending the eastern flank won’t be enough. Further, many of the drone sightings have been in civilian areas. Using hard military weapons, such as guns and anti-air missiles, would be exceedingly dangerous.

To truly protect against drones, the European Union and UK will likely need to develop new technologies, and assets may need to be deployed not just to military bases but also to civilian airports and other civilian infrastructure. Right now, it seems doubtful that Europe, and even the United States, has the technologies needed to fully counter the threat posed by drones. This is good news for defense companies, however, as demand for anti-drone technology to build a drone wall should result in research grants, purchases, and other forms of support.

EUAD Fundamentals and Valuation Comparison With PPA

I’ve already taken a long look at EUAD’s portfolio in an earlier analysis. While EUAD has steadily ticked up in the meantime, overall the portfolio’s basic attributes remain similar. For now, I’d like to take a closer look at EUAD in comparison to the SPDR S&P Aerospace & Defense ETF (XAR). Right now, EUAD measures up toe-to-toe quite well with XAR, but crucially, the United States' commitment to military spending has been consistent and generally trending up. As a result, I suspect more future upside is priced in and that American military companies will enjoy generally steady and predictable gains. EUAD, however, I believe will enjoy more substantial catalysts as Europe works to rearm.

XAR’s P/E ratio weighs in at 29.65, which is rather pricey but not surprising given the Trump Administration’s dedication to defense spending and the overall strained geopolitical environment. EUAD is a bit cheaper at 28.37, although the difference alone isn’t enough to move the needle much. Both XAR and EUAD have a P/S ratio of 2.65. According to Morningstar, EUAD offers a substantially better dividend yield of 1.19% to XAR’s .6%, a notable difference, although nothing overwhelming. Unfortunately, EUAD’s adjusted expense ratio of .5% is a bit higher than XAR’s attractive .35%. Still, EUAD isn’t particularly expensive as far as ETFs go.

Based on current market conditions, EUAD strikes me as fairly valued. There’s nothing that points to it being an exceptional bargain. Yet for my thesis, this is a favorable outcome. Crucially, Europe has talked about increasing military spending; they have committed to military spending, but they haven’t signed much yet, nor have they revealed many concrete plans. Most things, like the drone wall, seem more conceptual at the moment. EUAD is fairly valued right now, which means if Europe does increase military spending significantly and if they emphasize supporting the European defense industry, EUAD’s current fair value could quickly translate into (at today’s prices) an undervalued asset.

Ultimately, however, my case is very dependent on the thesis. When examining investments, ideally, I’ll find investments that appear to be at least a bit undervalued by today’s data while also being in a position to potentially catch strong emerging catalysts. An example of this among my recent recommendations was Intel (INTC). Months of negative pressure left Intel looking undervalued in early August. Even without a catalyst, Intel looked attractive. Then the Trump Administration stepped in to offer substantial support to Intel, and the stock soared, up more than 80% now.

EUAD as a whole likely won’t enjoy as strong a catalyst as that Intel bump. However, individual European defense contractors might enjoy big catalysts if major contracts, grants, and purchases are announced. If no major catalysts come into the picture in the near future, I suspect EUAD will continue to slowly tick up. For now, I believe EUAD investors can invest in a relatively safe investment based on current realities, while strong catalysts could spur substantial jumps.

Why I Could Be Wrong

Rearming is easier said than done, and many countries in Europe are facing myriad challenges, including high public debt, aging populations, and slow economic growth. Europe’s defense industry has declined over the years. Catching up will be no small feat, and there’s a risk that Europe will take its foot off the gas. This is especially true if the war in Ukraine were to end and if Russia were to cease intrusions and sabotage.

Even if Europe continues to rearm, we can’t take it for granted that Europe’s defense industry will be the big winners. European governments might decide instead to buy weapons from the United States, South Korea, or another country. Doing so may produce the best results at the best costs. Still, I believe Europe is serious about increasing self-sufficiency.

Key Takeaway: EUAD Continues to Look Like a Solid Investment

I believe the prospects for EUAD are good. The ETF is valued fairly now and in a position to enjoy strong catalysts in the future. Individual stocks will outperform, and so I plan to take a closer look at Thales, Airbus, and other companies in the near future. For now, EUAD will offer solid exposure to potentially powerful catalysts.

data.title: IXUS: Has The Makings Of An Ideal International Core Product
data.description:
Introduction to the iShares Core MSCI Total International Stock ETF

The iShares Core MSCI Total International Stock ETF (NASDAQ:IXUS), which is issued by BlackRock, Inc. under the ‘iShares’ brand, is a $50B fund (in terms of assets under management) that covers a wide pool (+4000) of international stocks outside the US. IXUS, which will complete 13 years on the bourses in October 2025, is priced at an expense ratio of 0.07% and offers a dividend yield of 2.82% at the current share price (IXUS’s distributions are made twice a year, in June and December).

What Does IXUS ETF Do?

The stocks that IXUS covers are based on the MSCI ACWI ex USA IMI Index [MAEUII], which the former pursues by way of representative sampling.

Representative sampling is a process through which an ETF only holds a subset of stocks from a certain index, which in aggregate, closely mimic the risk and return characteristics of the latter. Note that IXUS consists of over 4000 stocks, but MAEUII consists of over 6000 stocks.

Despite covering a narrower pool than MAEUII, we can infer that IXUS does a really good job of mimicking the former, as its tracking errors over different time durations (one, three, or five years) are very low at roughly 2%. In contrast, most ETFs witness tracking errors within a range of 7-9%. If you’re not familiar with the concept of tracking error, note that it is an integral tool in identifying how well a passively managed ETF is tracking its benchmark index. It basically tells you how different an ETF’s returns could be from the returns of its tracking index.

Now coming back to MAEUII, note that it is a very comprehensive global equity index, not just in terms of the number of stocks (+6000), but also in terms of the threshold of market cap that it covers (it covers large-, mid-, and small-cap stocks). Basically, in market-cap terms, it covers 99% of non-US global equities. Also note that MAEUII doesn’t just pick stocks from the non-US developed market [DM] pocket in isolation but also considers emerging markets [EM] as well. To elaborate, it fishes for stocks from 24 different EMs and 22 different DMs, so in effect you get stocks from almost 50 different international countries.

What Are The Main Characteristics Of IXUS’s Portfolio?

Even though IXUS is supposed to stand out for its wide reach (over 4000 global stocks), because it tracks an index that weights its holdings on the basis of their respective free-float market cap (the share price as a function of the number of shares available for trading), the portfolio ends up tilting towards the largest stocks in the globe. Giant-cap stocks account for almost half of IXUS’s portfolio, and including large-caps, they account for three-fourths. Meanwhile, small-cap exposure is quite minimal at less than 4%.

From a stylistic perspective, hybrid large-caps dominate (large-caps that are not prohibitive from a valuation perspective but also offer reasonable levels of sales and earnings growth), taking up almost one-third of the portfolio. After large-cap hybrid, large-cap value and large-cap growth have almost equal weights, taking up almost 25% of the portfolio in isolation. Broadly, this suggests from a stylistic angle this portfolio is quite well balanced, and investors don’t have to worry about missing out on any particular pocket.

From a sectoral angle, even though IXUS covers stocks from 11 different sectors, the top 50% of the portfolio is dominated by three cyclical sectors, particularly the financial sector.

From a regional perspective, Japanese stocks dominate this portfolio, taking up 15%; however, do also consider that there are quite a few Euro region nations (not including the UK), which in aggregate account for roughly 30% of the portfolio.

What Are The Chief Risks Associated With IXUS?

IXUS is denominated in USD, but its assets are denominated in non-USD currencies (mainly JPY, EUR, and GBP), making it vulnerable to potential dollar appreciation or foreign currency depreciation as it does not resort to hedging. That means, even if the foreign holdings of IXUS don’t move much and remain relatively flat over a period (say, one year), a potential appreciation of the USD vs. all these currencies (say, 5% on average) could end up damaging your returns by 5%

Even though “past performance is no guarantee of future results,” investors may also want to have some context on how poorly IXUS has underperformed US stocks, as well as global stocks (which also include US stocks), until now.
